narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 38, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many law school hopefuls would envy. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students in similar profiles. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 65% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. This stood out as the top driver, outweighing other strengths in the model's eyes. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar exam success by demonstrating the analytical skills that often correlate with passing. Your undergraduate GPA provided a moderate positive boost too, nudging the odds toward passing since it exceeds what's typical for successful bar takers. Overall, while your early grades and test scores shine, the dip in your Year 3 decile ranking dominated the decision, making your profile riskier than most admitted students whose rankings stay consistently higher. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, but rather how these metrics stacked up against historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted prep to boost your later academic deciles, perhaps through a post-bac program or additional certifications that highlight upward trends. Strengthening that area could flip similar models in your favor for future applications. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing such gaps—your LSAT and GPA give you a great starting point to reapply stronger next cycle. Keep pushing; admissions evolve, and so can your profile.
